content.text: Why Diagnosis Precedes Intervention

The most common mistake in GenAI adoption programmes is applying the same intervention to every barrier. Low activation in the Finance BU and low activation in the Legal BU look identical in the funnel — but Finance may be a skill barrier (people don't know how to write prompts for financial analysis tasks) while Legal may be a culture/manager barrier (legal leadership is cautious about AI use in client-facing work). Running a prompt workshop for Legal when the problem is management stance wastes time and generates resentment.

Match the intervention to the diagnosed barrier. A provisioning barrier requires IT coordination. A skill barrier requires champions and training. A trust barrier requires transparency and verification tools. A culture barrier requires management engagement. A workflow fit barrier may require an honest conversation about whether the tool is right for that role. None of these fixes work for the others.
This document presents the five-barrier taxonomy, the diagnostic signals that identify each type, and the Python model for scoring and routing interventions.
The Five-Barrier Taxonomy

attrs.alt: GenAI Adoption Barrier Taxonomy and Intervention Map

content.text: The five barrier types span the full adoption funnel. Access and Skill barriers tend to affect the Provisioned-to-Activated conversion; Trust and Culture barriers affect Activated-to-Retained; Workflow Fit affects Retained-to-Embedded. A group that has a Workflow Fit barrier will never reach the Embedded stage regardless of how good the onboarding is, because the tool simply does not match how that role works.
Diagnosing Barrier Type
The Two-Question Survey
The fastest diagnostic instrument is a two-question survey sent to non-activators (provisioned but not yet used) and churned users (activated then stopped):

'What is the main reason you haven't used [tool] for a real work task?' — multiple choice: (a) I can't access it / had technical problems; (b) I don't know how to get started; (c) I'm not confident the output will be good enough; (d) My manager prefers I don't use it yet; (e) I don't see how it would help with my actual tasks; (f) Other.
'If one thing would help you use it, what would it be?' — free text, 1 sentence.

The distribution of answers to question 1 maps directly to the barrier taxonomy: (a) = Access, (b) = Skill, (c) = Trust, (d) = Culture/Manager, (e) = Workflow Fit. Run the survey per BU so barriers are segmented, not averaged.
Intervention Design by Barrier Type
Access Barrier
Access barriers are the easiest to diagnose and the hardest to fix quickly, because the fix is almost always owned by IT and not the programme team. The intervention is escalation with a defined SLA:
Meet with the IT service desk lead to establish a provisioning SLA — five business days from request to access confirmation is a reasonable target.
Identify the specific step where requests are stalling (licence approval, device compatibility check, compliance module completion) and address that step, not the whole process.
For compliance module blockers: if the module is technically broken for certain device types, create a documented interim workaround rather than leaving users unable to proceed.
Publish a self-serve troubleshooting guide for the three most common access errors, reducing helpdesk load and enabling faster self-resolution.
Skill Barrier
Skill barriers are best addressed through
just-in-time, role-specific help
rather than repeat training. A user who attended a GenAI overview six weeks ago and still has not activated did not need more information — they needed a specific prompt to try on a specific task:

@dataclass
class SkillInterventionPlan:
    target_role: str
    target_bu: str
    non_activator_count: int
    # Intervention sequence
    step_1_champion_outreach: bool = True
    step_2_prompt_library_link: bool = True
    step_3_role_workshop: bool = False      # only if > 10 non-activators in role
    step_4_buddy_assignment: bool = False   # only if step 1-3 haven't worked in 14 days

    def recommended_sequence(self) -> list[str]:
        steps = [
            "Week 1: Champion sends personalised message with one role-specific prompt to try",
            "Week 1: Share link to prompt library filtered to this role's top 5 use cases",
        ]
        if self.non_activator_count > 10:
            steps.append("Week 2: Run 45-minute role-specific workshop (live demo, not slides)")
        steps.append("Week 3: Assign buddy to any still-non-activated users for 30-day support")
        return steps

plan = SkillInterventionPlan(
    target_role="Financial Analyst",
    target_bu="Finance BU",
    non_activator_count=18,
)
for step in plan.recommended_sequence():
    print(f"  - {step}")
Trust Barrier

Trust barriers are psychological, not technical, and cannot be addressed by repeating capability demonstrations. Users who do not trust the output have usually had a bad experience (their own or a colleague's) or have a professional liability concern about using AI output without full verification. The intervention addresses the specific fear:

Verification checklist: a one-page guide showing exactly how to verify AI output for common task types (fact-check a summary, validate a calculation, check a contract clause against the source). Having a checklist converts the open-ended anxiety of 'what if it's wrong?' into a tractable procedure.

Error recovery stories: champions proactively share cases where AI output was wrong, how they caught it, and what they did. This normalises error detection and signals that the culture rewards catching mistakes, not punishes them.

'AI as co-pilot' framing: explicitly reframe every communication as 'AI drafts, you decide'. A user who believes they are the decision-maker is more willing to use AI as a drafting tool than a user who believes approving an AI output makes them responsible for its accuracy.
Culture / Manager Barrier

Culture barriers are the most dangerous because they are often invisible in aggregate data — they suppress the entire team's adoption simultaneously, making it look like a provisioning or skill problem at the BU level. The diagnostic signal is a full team at near-zero activation while peer teams in the same BU are activating normally.
The intervention must address the manager, not the individual team members:

Manager briefing: a private 30-minute session with the specific manager covering (a) what the tool does and does not do, (b) the organisation's position on AI use for their role, (c) a concrete use case relevant to that manager's team that reduces risk rather than creating it.

Executive sponsor alignment: if the manager's caution stems from uncertainty about whether senior leadership actually wants the team using AI, a visible signal from the executive sponsor (direct message, inclusion in a town hall mention) resolves the ambiguity.

Peer-led quick win: identify one team member who is enthusiastic and facilitate them doing a five-minute demo at a team meeting — peer influence is more credible than programme communications for this barrier type.
Workflow Fit Barrier

Workflow fit is the barrier that requires the most intellectual honesty. Some roles genuinely do not have a strong fit with current GenAI tools. A manual data entry role, a highly specialised domain where AI hallucination risk is intolerable without expert review, or a role where all output goes directly to a high-accountability deliverable without the time for verification — these are not adoption failures.

The honest response to a Workflow Fit barrier is a use case discovery session. If the session produces two or three plausible fit points, pursue them. If it produces none, document the finding and stop measuring that role's non-adoption as a programme failure. Forcing adoption where there is no genuine fit destroys trust in both the tool and the programme.
Barrier Tracking Over Time

Barriers are not static. An Access barrier resolved in month one may reappear in month three if the provisioning process is inconsistently applied to new joiners. Track barrier type distribution quarterly as part of the adoption metrics review:
Quarter
Primary Barrier (Finance BU)
Primary Barrier (Legal BU)
Programme Action
Q1
ACCESS (IT provisioning delays)
ACCESS (same)
IT SLA established; compliance module simplified
Q2
SKILL (prompt writing)
CULTURE (manager stance)
Champion workshop for Finance; manager briefing for Legal
Q3
WORKFLOW_FIT (low-fit roles remain)
SKILL (manager stance resolved; now skill gap)
Use case discovery for Finance stragglers; prompt library for Legal
Q4
No dominant barrier (mixed)
TRUST (better skill, now concerned about output quality)
Verification checklist published for Legal

Each quarter's primary barrier informs the next quarter's intervention budget. A programme that repeats the same intervention quarter after quarter without checking whether the barrier has changed is spending money on the wrong problem.
Compound Barriers and Sequencing

Individual users — and entire teams — often face more than one barrier simultaneously. A Legal team might have both a Culture barrier (manager is cautious) and a Workflow Fit barrier (legal-specific use cases are not well represented in the prompt library). Attempting to fix the Workflow Fit barrier first is pointless if the manager will block adoption regardless. The intervention must be sequenced correctly: Culture first, then Workflow Fit.
Sequencing Rules
Access barriers are always first — no other intervention can succeed if the user cannot use the tool.
Culture/Manager barriers are second — if management actively discourages use, Skill and Trust interventions will not reach the team.
Skill and Trust barriers can be addressed in parallel — they do not depend on each other.

Workflow Fit is diagnosed last — many apparent Workflow Fit barriers dissolve once Skill improves (users who could not write effective prompts concluded the tool was not useful for their role, when the real problem was prompt quality).

The sprint plan makes compound barrier sequencing explicit. It prevents the common mistake of running all interventions simultaneously (which makes it impossible to determine which one worked) or addressing lower-priority barriers before higher-priority ones (which wastes the intervention resources entirely).
Measuring Intervention Effectiveness

Every intervention should have a pre-defined success metric measured 30 days after the intervention runs. Without this, the programme team cannot distinguish interventions that worked from interventions that coincided with a change that would have happened anyway.
Barrier Type
Intervention
30-Day Success Metric
Failure Signal
Access
IT SLA established; compliance module simplified
Provisioned% increased by >= 15 points
Provisioned% unchanged — check if a different step is now the blocker
Skill
Champion workshop + prompt library
Activation rate increased by >= 20 points among workshop attendees vs non-attendees
Activation rate flat — run user interviews; workshop content may have missed the actual gap
Trust
Verification checklist published; error-recovery session
Output utilisation rate increased by >= 10 points; 'concerned about mistakes' drops in next survey wave
Utilisation flat — trust may be driven by management concern (misclassified as Trust, actually Culture)
Culture
Manager briefing; exec sponsor message
Team activation rate moves from near-zero to >= 30%
Flat — manager may need direct OKR alignment, not just information
Workflow Fit
Use case discovery session
At least 2 concrete use cases identified and piloted
No fit found — stop measuring as failure; document and revisit in 6 months

from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class InterventionTracker:
    barrier_type: str
    intervention_date: date
    target_bu: str
    target_role: Optional[str]
    # Pre-intervention baseline
    baseline_metric_name: str
    baseline_metric_value: float
    # Post-intervention target (30 days later)
    target_improvement: float          # absolute percentage points
    # Post-intervention measurement (filled at day 30)
    post_metric_value: Optional[float] = None

    def days_since_intervention(self, as_of: date) -> int:
        return (as_of - self.intervention_date).days

    def ready_for_measurement(self, as_of: date) -> bool:
        return self.days_since_intervention(as_of) >= 30

    def outcome(self) -> Optional[str]:
        if self.post_metric_value is None:
            return None
        actual_improvement = self.post_metric_value - self.baseline_metric_value
        if actual_improvement >= self.target_improvement:
            return f"SUCCESS: {actual_improvement:+.1f}pp improvement (target {self.target_improvement:+.1f}pp)"
        if actual_improvement > 0:
            return f"PARTIAL: {actual_improvement:+.1f}pp improvement, short of {self.target_improvement:+.1f}pp target"
        return f"NO_EFFECT: {actual_improvement:+.1f}pp change — diagnose further"

tracker = InterventionTracker(
    barrier_type="SKILL",
    intervention_date=date(2025, 4, 1),
    target_bu="Finance BU",
    target_role="Financial Analyst",
    baseline_metric_name="activation_rate_pct",
    baseline_metric_value=32.0,
    target_improvement=20.0,
    post_metric_value=54.0,
)
print(tracker.outcome())
# SUCCESS: +22.0pp improvement (target +20.0pp)

Run the tracker for every intervention, not just the ones you expect to succeed. A No_Effect result is not a failure to hide — it is diagnostic data that eliminates a hypothesis and directs you to the actual root cause. The programme that learns fastest is the one that measures honestly.
When to Accept Non-Adoption
Not every non-adoption is a programme failure. Some populations genuinely should not be adopting current GenAI tools:

Roles with regulatory prohibition: certain financial services and legal roles have external regulatory constraints on AI use in client-facing work. These are not adoption barriers — they are correct non-adoption.

Roles where the tool creates more work than it saves: a role that spends 95% of its time in structured data systems (ERP, CRM) with no natural language drafting tasks may find no use case that saves meaningful time.
Roles in transition: a role that is being restructured or eliminated in the next six months is not the right target for a sustained adoption intervention.

Programme credibility depends on honest reporting. A programme that claims a 95% adoption target across all roles will be asked to explain every non-adopting individual. A programme that says 'target population: roles with strong use-case fit; current coverage: 87% of target population' is reporting a genuine success. Exclude the ineligible population from your denominator and document the exclusion rationale.
Summary
Adoption barriers fall into five types:
Access
(provisioning incomplete),
Skill
(don't know how to use it effectively),
Trust
(concerned about output quality or professional liability),
Culture/Manager
(management discourages or does not enable use), and
Workflow Fit
(the tool does not match the role's actual tasks). Each type has a distinctive diagnostic signal in funnel data and survey responses, and each requires a different intervention.

Diagnose before intervening: a skill intervention applied to a culture barrier wastes resources and generates frustration. Run a two-question survey per BU monthly, map answers to barrier types, and design interventions that address the specific barrier identified. Track barrier distribution quarterly — barriers shift as earlier ones are resolved, and the programme must shift with them.
